narrative:
Nydia Velazquez has represented parts of Brooklyn, Queens, and Lower Manhattan since 1992 — sixteen terms, thirty-three years, and counting down now that she has announced her 2026 retirement. The first Puerto Rican woman elected to Congress, Velazquez built her career as a champion of small business and a senior member of the Financial Services Committee, a dual perch that makes her record on the 80% Bill a fascinating study in progressive branding versus institutional comfort.

Start with stock trading: Velazquez voted for the STOCK Act in 2012 when it passed 417-2, which is a bit like bragging about showing up to a vote where the only controversy was who would be the two contrarians. She has not been a leading voice for strengthening the law or banning congressional trading outright. Her Financial Services Committee seat puts her in regular conversation with Wall Street — the securities and investment industry has been a consistent donor throughout her career, and the finance, insurance, and real estate sector has ranked among her top contributing industries cycle after cycle. She once memorably grilled Citigroup's CEO about his $24 million salary being 486 times the median employee wage, which is the kind of theater that plays well on C-SPAN but doesn't exactly threaten the donor pipeline.

On defense spending, Velazquez has been more genuinely progressive than many colleagues. She introduced amendments to the NDAA targeting military intervention in Latin America and co-led efforts to demilitarize police departments. But she has not been a prominent voice for Pentagon audits specifically, even as the Department of Defense has now failed seven consecutive audits while burning through nearly a trillion dollars annually.

Her consumer protection record is thin where the 80% Bill is thickest. No meaningful leadership on right-to-repair, corporate homebuying bans, or junk fee legislation — odd gaps for someone representing some of New York's most rent-burdened neighborhoods. She did introduce the Small Business Lending Disclosure Act to curb predatory lending, which aligns with her committee brand but sidesteps the broader corporate accountability agenda.

On campaign finance, Velazquez has supported Democratic reform vehicles in party-line fashion but has not co-sponsored the DISCLOSE Act or led on dark money reform. Corporate PACs have contributed to her campaigns throughout her career, and she has shown no interest in term limits — a position rendered moot only by her voluntary retirement after three decades. As for the lobbying revolving door, paid family leave, and Medicare drug negotiation, she has been a reliable caucus vote without distinguishing herself as a leader on any of these fronts.

Velazquez's legacy is real: she broke barriers, fought for Puerto Rico's self-determination, and built the Small Business Committee into a platform for entrepreneurs. But on the 80% Bill's core reforms, her record reads more like a progressive who got comfortable with the institution than one who tried to change it.

Chris Van Hollen has spent the better part of two decades in Washington doing something deeply unfashionable: trying to make the tax code fair. First as a congressman representing Maryland's 8th District and then, since 2017, as a U.S. Senator, Van Hollen has built a legislative career around the premise that billionaires should not pay a lower tax rate than nurses [1]. His Buffett Rule legislation — named after Warren Buffett's famous observation about his secretary's tax burden — became a touchstone for tax reform advocates and a recurring headache for colleagues who preferred that particular inequity remain unexamined [2]. His support for taxing the ultra-wealthy and closing tax loopholes aligns squarely with the 80% Bill's vision of a system where the rules apply to everyone, including those who can afford to rewrite them.

On campaign finance, Van Hollen has been one of the Senate's most persistent thorns in the side of dark money. He is the original Senate sponsor of the DISCLOSE Act, which would require super PACs and shadowy nonprofits to reveal their donors — a concept that polls at roughly 80% approval among voters and approximately 0% approval among the donors in question [3]. His push to ban corporate PACs and support fair elections reform reflects a consistent belief that the marketplace of ideas works poorly when some buyers can afford to purchase the entire marketplace [4]. He has reintroduced the DISCLOSE Act in virtually every Congress since Citizens United, displaying either admirable persistence or a masochistic streak, depending on one's perspective.

Van Hollen's policy portfolio extends well beyond fiscal matters. He has backed Medicare drug price negotiation, paid family leave, and the right to repair — the last of which takes the bold position that when you buy a tractor, you should be allowed to fix it [5]. His support for banning junk fees and corporate home buying reflects a senator who has noticed that his constituents are tired of being nickel-and-dimed by industries that have elevated hidden charges to an art form. On military and foreign policy, he has supported both the Pentagon audit and measures to end indefinite military authorizations, arguing that Congress should occasionally exercise its constitutional war powers rather than outsourcing them permanently to the executive branch [6].

On accountability and transparency, Van Hollen has backed the release of the Epstein files, lobbying bans for former officials, and police body camera mandates [7]. He supported Social Security funding protections and farmland preservation measures, understanding that a functioning safety net and a domestic food supply are not luxuries but prerequisites for a stable society. His alignment with the 80% Bill's twenty articles is substantial, though not without the occasional hedging that distinguishes a senator who must win statewide from a representative who can afford to be more surgical in their politics [8]. Still, in a chamber where ambition frequently outpaces conviction, Van Hollen has maintained a record that suggests he means most of what he says — which, by Senate standards, qualifies as practically revolutionary.

David Valadao is the Republican representing California's 22nd Congressional District, a heavily Democratic, majority-Latino stretch of the San Joaquin Valley where roughly one in seven residents works in agriculture and two-thirds rely on Medi-Cal for health coverage [1][2]. The son of Portuguese immigrants from the Azores, Valadao grew up on his family's dairy farm in Hanford, attended College of the Sequoias part-time, and joined the family business full-time -- eventually running two dairies and over a thousand acres of farmland before the whole operation collapsed under $13 million in debt, resulting in bankruptcy, a bank seizure, and an unpaid $325,000 settlement owed to workers who alleged they were denied breaks, minimum wage, and overtime [3][4][5]. He served one term in the California State Assembly before winning his House seat in 2012, lost it in 2018, and clawed it back in 2020. He sits on the powerful Appropriations Committee, chairing the Legislative Branch subcommittee and serving on the Agriculture and Defense subcommittees [6].

Valadao's most notable act of political courage was voting to impeach Donald Trump after January 6th, calling the incitement "un-American, abhorrent, and absolutely an impeachable offense" [7]. He is one of only two House Republicans who voted for impeachment and survived politically. That independent streak, however, has not extended to the 80% Bill's core reforms. Valadao has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, impose term limits, create a lifetime lobbying ban, audit the Pentagon, overturn Citizens United, or pass the DISCLOSE Act. On taxing the ultra-wealthy, he helped craft the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act -- from which the Center for American Progress estimated he personally stood to gain up to $1 million through a pass-through business loophole, while California's top one percent received average tax cuts of $26,890 compared to $774 for the working and middle class [8][9].

The gap between Valadao's rhetoric and his votes is widest on the issues his constituents care about most. He repeatedly promised he would "not support a final reconciliation bill that makes harmful cuts to Medicaid," then voted for the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which slashed Medicaid and food stamps [10][11]. Dozens of constituents protested outside his office; one told reporters "I feel betrayed" [12]. Valadao defended the vote by pointing to $1 billion for Western water storage and agricultural trade subsidies tucked into the bill -- real benefits for Central Valley farmers, but a curious consolation for the two-thirds of his district on Medi-Cal [2][13]. He also voted for a bill that critics say would let banks and credit card companies increase junk fees on consumers, undercutting his own cost-of-living messaging [14].

Follow the money and the picture sharpens. Valadao's top donor industries include vegetables, fruits, and tree nuts, home builders, and leadership PACs [15]. He exploited a leadership PAC loophole to funnel roughly $120,000 in extra campaign cash through mutual donation swaps with two dozen other GOP candidates -- part of a broader scheme in which 126 Republican House candidates converted more than $2.8 million via the same maneuver [16]. In 2024, cryptocurrency-linked super PACs spent $1.3 million supporting his reelection [17]. His Appropriations seat -- controlling the agricultural spending spigot -- makes him indispensable to the Valley's farming interests, which is precisely why the GOP tolerates a Trump-impeaching moderate in a blue district: he delivers for the donors who matter.

Valadao is the kind of congressman the 80% Bill was designed to expose. He has the independent brand -- the impeachment vote, the bipartisan photo ops, the water-infrastructure wins -- but on the structural reforms that eighty percent of Americans support, he is absent. No stock trading ban. No lobbying ban. No Pentagon audit. No dark money reform. He promised to protect Medicaid and voted to cut it. He championed a tax bill that enriched himself while his own dairy workers went unpaid. The contradiction is not subtle, and the voters of CA-22 are starting to notice.

Juan Vargas has represented the southern tip of California -- one of the poorest congressional districts in the state, hugging the Mexican border from San Diego to the Imperial Valley -- since 2013 [1]. A former Jesuit seminarian turned insurance executive turned state legislator, Vargas sits on the House Financial Services Committee, where he has carved out a niche on ESG disclosure and corporate governance reform [2]. He is a reliable Democratic vote on most kitchen-table issues. He is also, on the questions that matter most to the 80% Bill, a case study in how a member can align with the party on easy votes while quietly breaking ranks when powerful interests come calling.

On the surface, Vargas checks many of the 80% Bill boxes. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included the landmark Medicare drug price negotiation provision [3]. He supported the Build Back Better framework with its paid family leave and expanded social safety net provisions. He backed the For the People Act, which addressed gerrymandering, dark money, and campaign finance disclosure [4]. He has co-sponsored legislation on Social Security expansion and has spoken in favor of police accountability measures. On paper, this is a congressman who agrees with the American supermajority on the bread-and-butter progressive agenda.

But the cracks appear precisely where the money does. In March 2026, Vargas became one of only four House Democrats to vote against the Massie-Khanna war powers resolution, which would have required congressional authorization for the ongoing military engagement in Iran [5]. The resolution failed 212-219, with Vargas providing one of the critical Democratic defections that killed it. His stated rationale -- that "we need to have flexibility when service members are actively engaged" -- is the kind of blank check that the 80% Bill's AUMF repeal provision was designed to shred [6]. It is worth noting that all four Democrats who broke ranks on that vote share a common donor: AIPAC, which has poured over $155,000 into Vargas's campaigns over his career [7][8]. Vargas also voted in 2023 to provide an additional $14.3 billion for Israeli military operations, and earlier voted against a major antitrust package that would have cracked down on anti-competitive corporate behavior -- a curious position for a member who claims to fight for working families in one of the poorest districts in California [1]. On the congressional stock trading ban, Vargas has been conspicuously absent from the list of cosponsors on any of the major reform bills, despite sitting on the very committee that oversees financial markets [9].

Follow the money and the silence makes sense. Vargas's top contributing industry over his career is securities and investment, which has sent him $117,740 -- not a fortune by congressional standards, but a telling priority for a member whose district is defined by agricultural labor and cross-border commerce, not Wall Street [10]. His Financial Services Committee seat makes him a magnet for bank and insurance PAC money, and his campaign coffers reflect it. Early in his career, his political opponents flagged controversial payments to his brother-in-law, a lobbyist for the real estate industry, a connection that drew scrutiny during his initial congressional campaigns [1]. He has not championed the lifetime lobbying ban, the Pentagon audit, or term limits -- the kind of structural reforms that threaten the ecosystem that sustains his incumbency.

Vargas is the archetype of the go-along-to-get-along Democrat: right on the easy stuff, absent on the hard stuff, and reliably available when a critical vote needs to be quietly killed. His district deserves a representative who votes with the 80% supermajority on war powers and financial reform, not one who invokes "flexibility" as cover for doing exactly what his donors want.

Marc Veasey is that particular species of Democrat who votes with his party just often enough to keep the label, while spending the rest of his time making sure the fossil fuel industry knows he has not forgotten who built Fort Worth. He co-chairs the American Energy Dominance Caucus — yes, "Dominance" — alongside a Republican, advocating for what he calls an "all-of-the-above" energy strategy that, in practice, means oil, gas, and whatever renewables the oil and gas companies feel like investing in that quarter. He received $135,800 from oil and gas interests in the 2023-2024 cycle alone and has been documented holding fossil fuel investments while sitting on the Energy and Commerce Committee, which oversees the industry. The fox is not guarding the henhouse — the fox co-chairs a caucus celebrating henhouse dominance.

Veasey also sits on the Armed Services Committee, where he has been a tireless champion of the F-35 Joint Strike Fighter program, which happens to be manufactured by Lockheed Martin in his district. He has announced billions in defense contracts for Fort Worth with the enthusiasm of a car dealership grand opening, including a $7.8 billion deal for 71 F-35s and a $1.13 billion Army contract for Lockheed's missile division in Grand Prairie. He even co-introduced a House Resolution declaring the F-35 "a critical component of America's national security." When your district's largest employer is a defense contractor, your national security priorities tend to have a very specific mailing address.

To Veasey's credit on the 80% Bill scorecard, he voted with Biden 100% of the time in the 117th Congress, which means he supported Medicare drug price negotiation via the Inflation Reduction Act and voted for H.R. 1, the For the People Act, which included provisions for dark money disclosure, campaign finance reform, and fair elections. He has supported paid family leave legislation and has generally aligned with Democratic positions on taxing the ultra-wealthy and closing tax loopholes. His missed vote rate of 1.3% is better than the congressional median.

But the contradictions are impossible to ignore. Veasey backed the Keystone XL pipeline, championed fossil energy research reauthorization, and co-founded an "Energy Dominance" caucus whose very name was coined by the Trump administration. He announced in December 2025 that he would not seek reelection, briefly running for Tarrant County judge before suspending that campaign a week later — a political exit strategy with the decisiveness of a U-turn on a one-way street. On the policies where the public demands reform, Veasey has been half-right and half-bought, which in Congress still makes him above average. That is not the compliment it sounds like.

Jeff Van Drew is a dentist by training and a political contortionist by practice. He spent 30 years running a dental office in South Jersey, then served as a Democratic state senator before winning NJ-2 in 2018 [1]. One year later, facing cratering support among Democratic voters, he voted against both articles of Trump's first impeachment, walked into the Oval Office, and pledged his "undying support" to the president [2]. He officially switched to the Republican Party on January 7, 2020 [1]. The transformation was so rapid it should have come with a warning label.

The party switch was, in fairness, remarkably successful. Van Drew has won three elections as a Republican, most recently taking 58.1% in 2024 [3]. But his voting record still reveals the seams. Voteview rates him as more liberal than 98% of his Republican colleagues, and he has backed bills to remove Confederate statues from the Capitol and expand birth control access for veterans [1] -- votes that would get you excommunicated at most GOP caucus meetings.

The contradictions reached peak absurdity in May 2025 with the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. Van Drew publicly declared that Medicaid cuts in the bill were "political suicide" and said flatly, "I'm not voting for that" [4]. He signed a letter opposing the reductions. Then, on May 22, he voted for the bill anyway, along with all but one House Republican [5]. His explanation -- that he wanted to "move the budget process forward" but would oppose cuts later -- is the legislative equivalent of ordering dessert and promising to start the diet tomorrow. The bill also included an expansion of Section 199A, a tax provision that could personally benefit Van Drew, who reported over $5,000 in pass-through rental income [1].

His other signature cause is opposing offshore wind energy. Van Drew originally helped start the congressional Offshore Wind Caucus, then left it and became one of the industry's fiercest critics, arguing that turbines harm whales [1]. In 2025, he worked directly with President Trump to freeze offshore wind projects nationwide [6]. It is a position that happens to align with the fossil fuel donors who support his campaigns and with the real estate interests along the Jersey Shore.

Van Drew represents what happens when political survival becomes the ideology itself. He will say whatever the moment requires and vote however the party demands -- a skill he has practiced with the precision of a man who spent three decades drilling teeth.

Beth Van Duyne's political career is a masterclass in failing upward through the conservative media ecosystem. As mayor of Irving, Texas, she gained national attention in 2015 by crusading against a nonexistent "Sharia court" — a voluntary mediation panel that PolitiFact rated as a false claim. Two anti-Muslim hate groups, ACT for America and the Center for Security Policy, honored her with awards for the effort. From there it was a short hop to a Trump appointment at HUD, and then to Congress in 2020, where she won TX-24 by just 1.3 points. She now sits on the powerful Ways and Means Committee, where she chairs the Small Business Subcommittee on Economic Growth, Tax, and Capital Access — a title so long it practically needs its own zip code.

Van Duyne's campaign finance profile reads like a real estate industry catalog. The National Association of Realtors is her top contributor at $178,500 over her career, with the real estate sector delivering $352,333 overall. Her funding breaks down to 40% from PACs, just under 3% from small donors, and the rest from large individual contributions — a ratio that suggests her grassroots support could fit in a windowsill planter. AIPAC, American Airlines, and the Sam Pack Auto Group round out her top backers.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Van Duyne has been a reliable opponent of virtually every reform Americans overwhelmingly support. She voted against H.R. 1, the For the People Act, which would have enacted sweeping election reform including dark money disclosure, campaign finance limits, and anti-gerrymandering provisions. She called it the "destroy election integrity and centralize all power in Washington Act" — which is quite a description for a bill that would have required her donors to identify themselves publicly. She voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation. She has no record supporting a congressional stock trading ban, term limits, Pentagon audits, or paid family leave.

She did, however, vote to overturn the 2020 election results in Arizona and Pennsylvania on January 6, 2021, just hours after the Capitol attack. She has since focused her legislative energy on immigration enforcement, introducing the No Aid for Illegal Entry Act and the American Entrepreneurs First Act to exclude refugees from SBA loans — policies that play well on cable news but appear nowhere in the list of things 80% of Americans are asking for.

Van Duyne's journey from Islamophobic mayor to corporate-funded congresswoman is a reminder that in American politics, the path to power does not require solving problems — it requires finding the right people to blame for them. Her donors are happy. Her voters may want to ask why.

Derrick Van Orden is a retired Navy SEAL, occasional actor, best-selling author, and the angriest man in Congress, which in the current House Republican Conference is genuinely saying something. Since arriving in Washington in 2023, Van Orden has compiled a highlight reel of public outbursts that would get a Little League coach banned from the bleachers.

In July 2023, Van Orden stumbled upon Senate pages -- high schoolers in their final week of service -- lying on the Capitol Rotunda floor taking photos of the dome ceiling, a cherished tradition. Van Orden called the teenagers "jackasses" and "pieces of s---," told them he did not "give a f--- who you are," and then refused to apologize, insisting he was protecting the integrity of the Rotunda. Bipartisan rebuke followed from both chambers. Before that, in 2021, he barged into the Prairie du Chien Memorial Library, aggressively shoving books around a Pride Month display, and berated a 17-year-old library page so badly that the girl told her parents she no longer felt safe at work. Then in October 2023, Van Orden launched an obscenity-laden tirade at senior Biden administration officials during a classified Israel-Hamas briefing, confronting the acting deputy secretary of state and other officials with shouted profanities. He later denied using any vulgarity, because apparently everyone else in the room was hallucinating.

When he is not terrorizing teenagers and diplomats, Van Orden serves on the Agriculture, Armed Services, and Veterans' Affairs committees. He leans hard on his SEAL credentials -- he starred in the 2012 film Act of Valor and wrote A Book of Man: A Navy SEAL's Guide to the Lost Art of Manhood, a title that aged like milk given his documented habit of screaming at children. His campaign has been heavily funded by agribusiness interests and right-wing megadonor Richard Uihlein, with nearly $6.6 million raised in the 2024 cycle, much of it transferred from House GOP leadership PACs like Tom Emmer's Majority Builders and Mike Johnson's Grow the Majority.

Van Orden attended the January 6 rally preceding the Capitol attack. He later said he left when the protest became a mob, and he has not been charged, though a photo may show him beyond police lines. He voted for the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which slashed Medicaid and food assistance while extending tax cuts for the wealthy.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Van Orden has taken no meaningful action on stock trading bans, lobbying bans, dark money disclosure, Pentagon audits, paid family leave, term limits, corporate PAC bans, or campaign finance reform. He represents western Wisconsin's dairy farms and small towns, but his donor list and voting record serve the GOP leadership apparatus that bankrolled his seat. The man who wrote a guide to manhood cannot manage basic civility with teenagers. The 80% Bill asks representatives to fight for the people -- Van Orden mostly fights with them.

Gabe Vasquez is the kind of candidate swing-district strategists dream about on a whiteboard. A first-generation Mexican-American who grew up splitting time between El Paso and Ciudad Juarez, he worked in wildlife conservation before winning a Las Cruces City Council seat with over 70% of the vote [1]. In 2022 he unseated Republican incumbent Yvette Herrell — one of the House's top recipients of oil and gas money — by just 1,350 votes in a newly redrawn NM-2 [2]. He survived the rematch in 2024, cementing himself as a Democrat who can hold a district that runs from Albuquerque's Westside to the Permian Basin borderlands [3].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Vasquez's record is a study in careful positioning. He co-sponsored the bipartisan Restore Trust in Congress Act to ban stock trading by members of Congress and their families [4]. He co-introduced the Families First Housing Act, giving families and nonprofits first dibs on federally-connected homes before Wall Street investors, and backed the Stop Wall Street Landlords Act to strip tax breaks from large investor-owned entities [5]. He co-sponsored both the John R. Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act and the Freedom to Vote Act, the twin pillars of the 80% Bill's fair-elections plank [6]. And his Keep Mobile Homes Affordable Act targets corporate landlords who buy up mobile home parks and jack up lot rents on some of the country's most vulnerable homeowners [7].

Where it gets interesting is at the Agriculture Committee table. Vasquez was one of just seven Democrats to vote the Republican-led Farm, Food, and National Security Act of 2026 out of committee after a 22-hour markup [8]. That kind of bipartisan crossover plays well in a district where ranchers and chile farmers need the Farm Bill more than they need partisan purity — but it also means Vasquez will face questions about what was traded away in the process. He co-chairs the Bipartisan Public Lands Caucus alongside Republican Ryan Zinke, and has led border security bills like the Stop Fentanyl at the Border Act [9], further burnishing a centrist brand that keeps progressive watchdogs squinting.

The money tells a familiar swing-district story. In 2024, 62% of his fundraising came from large individual contributions, with PACs providing roughly 19% [10]. JStreetPAC was his biggest outside backer. He replaced a member bankrolled by Big Oil, but NM-2 still sits atop the Permian Basin — meaning Vasquez must thread the needle between climate commitments and an economy that runs on extraction. So far, his legislative choices suggest a representative who genuinely backs many 80% Bill priorities but picks his fights with an actuarial eye on the next election.

Eugene Vindman rode into Congress on one of the most compelling origin stories in modern politics: the whistleblower's brother, the Army JAG officer who stood up to a president, the man who sacrificed his military career for democracy. It's a narrative so cinematic that voters in Virginia's 7th District could be forgiven for not reading the fine print.

And there is quite a bit of fine print. Shortly after arriving on Capitol Hill, Vindman landed seats on the Armed Services and Agriculture Committees -- a sensible pairing for a veteran representing a district with rural stretches and nearly 60,000 federal workers. He signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge, which earned him a thank-you billboard in his district. He co-sponsored a junk fees transparency bill. He even voted for a bipartisan War Powers Resolution to check executive military authority. On paper, the man is a populist reformer's dream.

But the dream gets complicated when you follow the money trail to Kyiv. The Pentagon's own General Counsel asked the House Ethics Committee to investigate Vindman and his twin brother Alexander over Trident Support LLC, a consulting firm the brothers founded to broker deals between American defense companies and the Ukrainian government. Financial disclosures show Vindman pulled in over $200,000 from Trident across two years, with the firm valued between $500,000 and $1 million. The Pentagon found no evidence either brother obtained the required pre-approval from the Secretary of the Army or State before offering defense services to a foreign government. Vindman insists he "never took a dime from a Ukrainian citizen or government entity, directly or indirectly," which is technically possible when you're the middleman connecting American contractors to Ukrainian buyers.

For a member of the New Democrat Coalition -- the caucus that prides itself on pragmatic, pro-business centrism -- Vindman's legislative priorities read like a focus-grouped checklist of Things Democrats Should Say. He champions federal worker protections (smart, given his district), military family support, and lower prescription drug costs. What you won't find him championing with equal vigor are the structural reforms that would actually threaten the system he now inhabits: banning congressional stock trading (no co-sponsorship found), auditing the Pentagon he once served (and now oversees on Armed Services), or confronting the defense industry whose contractors he once brokered deals for.

The irony is rich enough to choke on: a man celebrated for blowing the whistle on corruption now faces an ethics investigation over his own pre-congressional business dealings. His $3.8 million campaign war chest suggests plenty of donors believe the hero narrative outweighs the Trident footnote. Whether Virginia's voters will read that footnote before 2026 remains the only real suspense in this particular sequel.
